questions.text: When we dissolve a compound of transition element in a solution of salt then it will form:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: When a compound of a transition element is dissolved in a solution of salt, it usually does not form simple ions. Instead, it can form complex ions, as I mentioned earlier. The formation of complex ions is due to the ability of transition metals to form coordinate covalent bonds with ligands, which are typically molecules or ions that can donate electrons to the metal ion. The resulting complex ions have a net charge and can have a variety of shapes and structures, depending on the specific compound and the ligands involved. The formation of complex ions is an important characteristic of transition metals that makes them useful in a wide range of applications, including catalysis, biochemistry, and materials science.

When a compound of a transition element is dissolved in a solution of salt, it generally does not form strong anions. Instead, it can form complex ions, depending on the specific compound and the conditions of the solution. The type of ion that forms will depend on the nature of the transition element and the ligands present in the solution. If the transition element is in a high oxidation state, it may form complex ions with ligands that have a high affinity for electrons, resulting in the formation of strong anions. However, this is not always the case, and the formation of strong anions is not a general characteristic of transition elements.

When a compound of a transition element is dissolved in a solution of salt, it generally does not form double salts. Instead, it can form complex ions, depending on the specific compound and the conditions of the solution. Double salts are a specific type of compound that contains two different salts in a single crystal structure. They are formed when two different salts crystallize together from a solution, and they have a unique set of properties that differ from those of the individual salts. However, the formation of double salts is not a general characteristic of transition elements, and it depends on the specific conditions of the solution and the nature of the salts involved.

When a compound of a transition element is dissolved in a solution of salt, it will form complex ions. Complex ions are formed when a transition metal ion is surrounded by ligands, which are typically molecules or ions that can donate electrons to the metal ion. The ligands form coordinate covalent bonds with the metal ion, and this results in the formation of a complex ion with a net charge. The type of complex ion that forms will depend on the specific compound and the conditions of the solution, such as the pH and the presence of other ions. The ability of transition elements to form complex ions is an important characteristic that makes them useful in a wide range of applications, including catalysis, biochemistry, and materials science.

questions.explanation: When a compound of a transition element is dissolved in a solution of salt, it will form complex ions. Complex ions are formed when a transition metal ion is surrounded by ligands, which are typically molecules or ions that can donate electrons to the metal ion. The ligands form coordinate covalent bonds with the metal ion, and this results in the formation of a complex ion with a net charge. The type of complex ion that forms will depend on the specific compound and the conditions of the solution, such as the pH and the presence of other ions. The ability of transition elements to form complex ions is an important characteristic that makes them useful in a wide range of applications, including catalysis, biochemistry, and materials science.

questions.text: More energy is required to remove an electron from _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In an atom, if a subshell (orbital) is half-filled, it means that it contains one electron in each orbital of that subshell. Such a configuration has greater stability compared to other electron configurations, which leads to a stronger binding force between the electrons and the nucleus. As a result, more energy is required to remove an electron from a half-filled subshell compared to a partially filled or completely filled subshell.

When a subshell is completely filled, it means that all the available orbitals in that subshell have two electrons each, following the Pauli exclusion principle. This configuration also results in greater stability due to minimized electron repulsion. As a result, more energy is required to remove an electron from a completely filled subshell compared to a partially filled subshell.

A partially filled subshell contains some, but not all, of the available electrons in that subshell. Such configurations are generally less stable than half-filled or completely filled subshells. As a result, less energy is required to remove an electron from a partially filled subshell compared to a half-filled or completely filled subshell.

As per Hund's rule, atoms with half-filled or completely-filled orbitals are comparably more stable, so removing an electron from such atoms requires more energy.The orbitals in which the sub-shell is exactly half-filled or completely filled are more stable because of the symmetrical distribution of electrons.

questions.explanation: As per Hund's rule, atoms with half-filled or completely-filled orbitals are comparably more stable, so removing an electron from such atoms requires more energy.The orbitals in which the sub-shell is exactly half-filled or completely filled are more stable because of the symmetrical distribution of electrons.

questions.text: The color of transition metal complexes is due to transition of electrons between:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: As per the explanation, this option is not correct.

When transition metals form complex ions or compounds, the d orbitals split into two energy levels. Electrons from the lower energy level absorb light of wavelength corresponding to the visible region in the EM spectrum and get excited to a higher energy level. The remaining unabsorbed wavelength is reflected and that gives the characteristic colour to the complex. As the energy difference between the high and lower energy levels of electrons varies for different complexes, the colour also varies. As discussed, the splitting occurs in d-orbital and transition of electrons between the two energy levels (d-d transition) is responsible for imparting different colours. Therefore, option B is correct.
As per the explanation, this option is not correct.
As per the explanation, this option is not correct.

questions.explanation: The color of transition metal complexes is due to the transition of electrons between different energy levels within the d orbitals of the metal ion. The d orbitals have different energy levels and can accommodate a varying number of electrons. When a transition metal ion forms a complex with ligands (atoms, ions, or molecules that surround the metal ion), the d orbitals split into different energy levels due to the influence of the ligands. This splitting is known as crystal field splitting. The energy difference between these split d orbitals corresponds to the energy of visible light. When white light passes through a colored solution containing transition metal complexes, certain wavelengths of light are absorbed by the electrons in the d orbitals, causing them to transition from a lower energy level to a higher energy level. The absorbed wavelengths are subtracted from the white light, resulting in the complementary color being observed. The specific color observed depends on the energy difference between the split d orbitals, which is influenced by factors such as the nature of the ligands and the oxidation state of the metal ion. Different ligands can induce different degrees of splitting, leading to variations in color. For example, complexes with ligands that create larger energy differences between the d orbitals tend to absorb longer wavelengths (lower energy) of light and appear yellow or red. Conversely, complexes with ligands that create smaller energy differences tend to absorb shorter wavelengths (higher energy) of light and appear blue or green. Overall, the absorption of specific wavelengths of light by transition metal complexes due to electron transitions within the d orbitals is responsible for their characteristic colors. When transition metals form complex ions or compounds, the d orbitals split into two energy levels. Electrons from the lower energy level absorb light of wavelength corresponding to the visible region in the EM spectrum and get excited to a higher energy level. The remaining unabsorbed wavelength is reflected and that gives the characteristic colour to the complex. As the energy difference between the high and lower energy levels of electrons varies for different complexes, the colour also varies. As discussed, the splitting occurs in d-orbital and transition of electrons between the two energy levels (d-d transition) is responsible for imparting different colours. Therefore option B is correct. (Source: PCTB book 2, page 102)

Electronic configuration of Option D is as follows:Option D, Cu2+: 1s2 2s2 2p6 3s2 3p6 3d9, so its d subshell is not filled completely, one orbital is partially filled, so it is the correct answer.

questions.text: EDTA ion is a _ ligand.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A monodentate ligand donates only one pair of electrons to a central metal atom. EDTA is not monodentate because it can donate more than one pair of electrons.
A bidentate ligand can donate two pairs of electrons to a central metal atom. However, EDTA can donate more than two pairs of electrons, making it more than bidentate.
A tridentate ligand donates three pairs of electrons to a central metal atom. EDTA is capable of donating more than three pairs, so it is not tridentate.
EDTA is a polydentate ligand as it can form multiple bonds with a central metal ion. Specifically, it is a hexadentate ligand, meaning it can donate six pairs of electrons from its donor atoms.
This option is incorrect because the correct answer is Option D, which states that EDTA is a polydentate ligand.

questions.explanation: EDTA ion is polydentate. Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) is an amino polycarboxylic acid with the formula [CH2N(CH2CO2H)2]2. This white, water-soluble solid is widely used to bind to iron and calcium ions. It binds these ions as a hexadentate ("six-toothed") chelating agent.

questions.text: Which of the following has the maximum number of unpaired d electrons?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Mg2+ has the electron configuration 1s2 2s2 2p6. It lacks d electrons entirely, resulting in no unpaired d electrons.
Ti3+ has the electron configuration [Ar] 3d1, meaning it contains only one unpaired d electron.
V3+ has the electron configuration [Ar] 3d2. It has two unpaired d electrons, following Hund's rule of maximum multiplicity.
Fe2+ has the electron configuration [Ar] 3d6. According to Hund's rule, it has four unpaired d electrons, making it the option with the most unpaired d electrons among the given choices.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Fe2+. The electron configuration for Fe2+ is [Ar] 3d6, with the 3d subshell holding six electrons: four of these are unpaired due to Hund's rule, which states that electrons fill degenerate orbitals singly first. Mg2+ lacks d electrons entirely, Ti3+ has only one unpaired d electron ([Ar] 3d1), and V3+ has two unpaired d electrons ([Ar] 3d2).

questions.text: The transition element which doesn't show variable valency?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Copper is a transition metal that exhibits variable valency. It commonly shows a valency of +1 or +2, but it can also have other valencies such as +3 and +4 in certain compounds.
Scandium is a transition metal that typically exhibits a valency of +3. However, it can also show a valency of +2 in some compounds, such as scandium(II) chloride (ScCl2).

Zinc is a transition metal that exhibits a fixed valency of +2 in most of its compounds. It does not commonly show variable valency like other transition elements. For example, zinc oxide (ZnO) and zinc sulfate (ZnSO4) both involve zinc with a valency of +2. So, this option is correct.

Chromium is a transition metal that commonly shows variable valency. It can have valencies ranging from -2 to +6, with the most stable and commonly observed valencies being +2, +3, and +6. For example, in chromium(III) chloride (CrCl3), chromium exhibits a valency of +3.

questions.explanation: The answer to the question should be option C because Zinc does not show the variable valency as elements of the d-block. After all, the d-orbital is full. Its outer electronic configuration is 3d^10 4s^2 So, they tend to lose only 2 electrons from its outermost shell. Hence, only show a valency of +2 and no variable valency.

questions.text: Valence electronic configuration Cu+2 is (29Cu)
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: As per the explanation, this option is incorrect.

Cu is an element of d-block. Its electronic configuration is anomalous owing to the stability associated with half filled and as in the case of Cu, completely filled d-subshell. One electron from the 4s subshell jumps to 3d subshell, thereby making the electronic configuration:1s2, 2s2, 2p6, 3s2, 3p6, 4s1, 3d10Now, for the formation of Cu2+ ion, the Cu atom must lose 2 electrons from the highest energy level. The important thing here is that the 4s orbital of an atom usually fills before electrons go into its 3d orbitals because the 4s orbital is slightly lower in energy than the empty 3d orbitals (n+l rule). However, once electrons are in the 3d orbital, the 4s is no longer lower in energy, it becomes higher in energy than 3d so when ionisation occurs electrons are lost from the 4s orbital before they're lost from the 3d orbitals.Hence, the first electron is lost from 4s and the one after that, from 3d. So the new electronic configuration from Cu2+ is:1s2, 2s2, 2p6, 3s2, 3p6, 4s0, 3d9Therefore, option B is the correct answer.
As per the explanation, this option is incorrect.
As per the explanation, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The valence electronic configuration of Cu+2 is derived from the neutral atom of copper (Cu), which has an atomic number of 29. The electronic configuration of neutral copper (Cu) is 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ 4s¹ 3d¹⁰. When copper loses two electrons to form the Cu+2 ion, the two outermost electrons from the 4s orbital are removed. Therefore, the valence electronic configuration of Cu+2 is 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ 3d⁹.Cu is an element of d-block. Its electronic configuration is anomalous owing to the stability associated with half filled and as in the case of Cu, completely filled d-subshell. One electron from the 4s subshell jumps to 3d subshell, thereby making the electronic configuration:1s2, 2s2, 2p6, 3s2, 3p6, 4s1, 3d10Now, for the formation of Cu2+ ion, the Cu atom must lose 2 electrons from the highest energy level. The important thing here is that the 4s orbital of an atom usually fills before electrons go into its 3d orbitals because the 4s orbital is slightly lower in energy than the empty 3d orbitals (n+l rule). However, once electrons are in the 3d orbital, the 4s is no longer lower in energy, it becomes higher in energy than 3d so when ionisation occurs electrons are lost from the 4s orbital before they're lost from the 3d orbitals.Hence, the first electron is lost from 4s and the one after that, from 3d. So the new electronic configuration from Cu2+ is:1s2, 2s2, 2p6, 3s2, 3p6, 4s0, 3d9Therefore, option B is the correct answer.

questions.text: Scandium, Sc, is the first of the 3d blocks of elements in the Periodic table.Which properties of Sc approve the above fact?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This statement is not directly related to the fact that Sc is the first of the 3d block elements. The formation of ionic chloride compounds is a general characteristic of many elements, but it does not specifically support the placement of Sc in the 3d block.

options.text: Sc readily forms oxidation state of +3.
options.explanation: Sc readily forms an oxidation state of +3. This statement supports the fact that Sc is the first element in the 3d block of elements. The 3d block consists of transition metals, which commonly exhibit multiple oxidation states. Scandium readily forms the +3 oxidation state, which is typical for elements in the 3d block.

options.text: Compounds containing the Sc3+ ion are always colored.
options.explanation: Compounds containing the Sc3+ ion are always coloured. This statement is not directly related to the fact that Sc is the first of the 3d block elements. The color of compounds containing Sc3+ ions depends on various factors, including the ligands bound to the Sc3+ ion and the electronic transitions within the compound. It is not a defining characteristic of Sc's position in the 3d block.

options.text: Sc has ionic chloride and Sc readily forms oxidation state of +3
options.explanation: Scandium is the first element of the 3d block of transition metals, which means it has a partially filled d orbital. Some properties of Sc that support this include its ability to form stable compounds in the +3 oxidation state and its ionic chloride, which is formed due to the transfer of an electron from Sc to Cl.

questions.explanation: Scandium is the first element of the 3d block of transition metals, which means it has a partially filled d orbital. Some properties of Sc that support this include its ability to form stable compounds in the +3 oxidation state and its ionic chloride, which is formed due to the transfer of an electron from Sc to Cl.

questions.subject: Chemistry

Iron is not typically associated with poisoning when bullets are left in the body.
Arsenic is not commonly found in bullets and is not a typical concern when bullets are not removed from the body.

questions.explanation: Mostly, bullets are made of lead which is toxic. Lead bullet fragments left in contact with body fluids are capable of solubilizing lead.

questions.text: The chemical name of K2[Zn(OH)4] is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Incorrect. The term 'zincate' should be used instead of 'zinc' to indicate the presence of a complex ion structure.

Correct. The compound consists of a potassium ion and a complex ion where zinc has an oxidation state of +2. The use of 'tetrahydroxozincate' correctly indicates the presence of four hydroxide ions in the coordination sphere.
Incorrect. The oxidation state of zinc in this compound is +2, not +6. The Roman numeral must accurately reflect the oxidation state.
Incorrect. This name suggests only one hydroxide ion is present, which contradicts the formula indicating four hydroxide ions.

questions.explanation: The chemical name of K2[Zn(OH)4] is derived by naming the cation first, which is Potassium. The complex ion is named by first identifying the central metal, Zinc, which has an oxidation state of +2, indicated in Roman numerals as (II). The presence of four hydroxide ions is denoted by 'tetrahydroxo'. Therefore, the correct name for the compound is Potassium tetrahydroxozincate (II), reflecting both the coordination number and the oxidation state of zinc. Other options are incorrect because they either misrepresent the oxidation state or the number of hydroxide ions in the compound.

questions.text: Paramagnetic elements contain:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Elements with all paired electrons are diamagnetic, not paramagnetic, as they do not have unpaired electrons to create a magnetic moment.
While having unpaired electrons is a characteristic of paramagnetic materials, it is not necessarily true that all electrons are unpaired. Even one unpaired electron can result in paramagnetism.
Paramagnetism occurs when an element has one or more unpaired electrons, which causes a net magnetic moment and attraction to a magnetic field.
This refers to ions rather than paramagnetic elements. The number of electrons and protons does not determine paramagnetism directly.

questions.explanation: Paramagnetic elements are those with one or more unpaired electrons. These unpaired electrons create a net magnetic moment, making the element attracted to an external magnetic field. Option C is correct because it acknowledges the presence of unpaired electrons, which is the primary cause of paramagnetism. Option A is incorrect because paired electrons result in diamagnetism, not paramagnetism. Option B is misleading as it implies that all electrons must be unpaired for paramagnetism, which is not true. Even one unpaired electron can result in paramagnetism. Option D is unrelated to paramagnetism; it describes ions, which have an imbalance of electrons and protons, resulting in a charge.

questions.text: Choose the correct IUPAC name for the following complex.[Cr(NH3)4Cl2]Cl
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option incorrectly uses 'trichloro' instead of 'dichloro' and does not follow the correct order of naming ligands.
This is the correct IUPAC name. The complex has four ammine (NH3) ligands and two chloro (Cl) ligands. The oxidation state of chromium is three, indicated by (III).
This option incorrectly separates 'Tetra' and 'ammonia' and does not follow the standard IUPAC order of naming ligands.
This option incorrectly uses 'chromate' instead of 'chromium' and does not follow the correct order for naming ligands.

questions.explanation: The correct IUPAC name for the complex [Cr(NH3)4Cl2]Cl is Tetraamminedichlorochromium (III) chloride. The complex contains four ammine (NH3) ligands and two chloro (Cl) ligands, which are named in alphabetical order as 'tetraammine' and 'dichloro'. The oxidation state of the central metal ion, chromium, is +3, denoted as (III). The name ends with 'chloride' to indicate the presence of the counter ion Cl- outside the coordination sphere. Other options do not follow the IUPAC rules for naming coordination complexes, either by using incorrect prefixes, separating terms incorrectly, or misnaming the metal.
